item.title: FortiSandbox, n8n, Cisco SD-WAN Exploitation and APT37 NarwhalRAT Activity
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally...
item.content:encoded:
During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally reachable workflow platforms, alongside regulatory pressure to produce incident context quickly. Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws were reported as under active exploitation, with Defused observing attacks against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in the prior 24 hours. Fortinet had issued fixes for the three critical-severity vulnerabilities on April 14; the flaws allow unauthenticated attackers to escalate privileges and execute unauthorized code via low-complexity command injection without user interaction. Fortinet also previously flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal issue, as exploited in the wild. Cisco released updates for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw stems from insufficient validation during file upload and can allow an authenticated remote attacker with write access to create or overwrite files, potentially escalating privileges to root. CISA added the issue to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a June 29, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cyera disclosed CVE-2026-21858, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability affecting locally deployed n8n instances. The issue arises from Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks, allowing attacker-controlled JSON to override trusted file metadata and copy local files such as n8n’s SQLite database or configuration into retrievable workflow data. The researchers described how those files could enable forged administrator sessions and subsequent command execution through n8n’s Execute Command node. CyCognito reported expanding external attack surface analysis to more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton. The company described cases involving an exposed MCP server permitting unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data and a public CrewAI stack where the agent API was authenticated but the RAG knowledge base remained exposed. Silent Push separately framed NIS2’s 24-hour early warning requirement as dependent on rapid infrastructure context, arguing that DNS, WHOIS, certificate, host scan, traffic, and behavioral relationship data are increasingly central to timely incident characterization. ESET reported Windows variants of SprySOCKS, previously associated with Linux malware, used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The activity was attributed with high confidence to Earth Lusca, also tracked as FishMonger and known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22. The Windows variants, WINDRV and WINPLUS, support TCP, UDP, and WebSocket communications, more than 30 C2 commands, SOCKS proxying, process and file management, and collection of keystrokes, clipboard contents, and active window titles. Separately, Genians reported that North Korean group ScarCruft, or APT37, used spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security alerts to deliver NarwhalRAT. The emails referenced abnormal one-time password activity and carried ZIP archives containing malicious LNK files, which initiated a multi-stage infection chain involving batch scripts, Python retrieval, and scheduled-task persistence. NarwhalRAT supports keystroke logging, screenshots, ambient audio recording, USB data collection, directory uploads, C2 command execution, and C2 switching via Korean website relays and pCloud API functionality. Kaspersky reported malicious Wallpaper Engine packages distributed through Steam Workshop since late 2025. The campaign abused Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds, and some malicious wallpapers had thousands or tens of thousands of downloads. One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe, used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate and hijack live Steam sessions, and exfiltrated data to 120.48.156[.]17. iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach involving patient personal and health information stored in third-party-hosted business applications. The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claimed possession of sensitive data, and demanded payment to prevent disclosure. iRhythm attributed access to social engineering and said it had no evidence that products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution, or financial reporting systems were affected.

Critical Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting multiple critical Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities that allow unauthenticated remote code execution or privilege escalation. Administrators should upgrade affected FortiSandbox deployments immediately to block incoming attacks.

  • Defused reported that attackers have been exploiting multiple Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities in the past 24 hours, including CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089.
  • Fortinet released security updates for CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 on April 14.
  • The three critical-severity FortiSandbox flaws allow unauthenticated threat actors to escalate privileges and remotely execute unauthorized code through low-complexity command injection attacks that require no user interaction.
  • Fortinet also flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal vulnerability, as exploited in the wild in April.
  • CISA tracks 26 Fortinet vulnerabilities exploited in attacks in recent years, including 13 abused by ransomware gangs.

Ni8mare - Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in n8n (CVE-2026-21858) — Must Read

Researchers found CVE-2026-21858, a critical unauthenticated n8n flaw caused by Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks that can let attackers read local files, forge admin sessions, and achieve remote code execution. Locally deployed n8n users should upgrade to version 1.121.0 or later immediately.

  • Researchers discovered CVE-2026-21858, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in n8n with a CVSS score of 10.0 that affects locally deployed instances.
  • The vulnerability stems from Content-Type confusion in n8n Form webhooks, where a request parsed as application/json can override the req.body.files object that file-handling code later trusts.
  • By controlling req.body.files[number].filepath, an attacker can cause n8n to copy local files such as /etc/passwd, /home/node/.n8n/database.sqlite, or /home/node/.n8n/config into a workflow for later retrieval.
  • The article explains that reading the local database and configuration file can provide the data needed to forge an n8n-auth session cookie and log in as an administrator.
  • After gaining administrator access, an attacker can create a workflow using n8n’s Execute Command node to achieve code execution on the instance.

Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw — Must Read

Cisco released fixes for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that lets authenticated attackers with write access create or overwrite files and potentially gain root privileges. CISA added the vulnerability to its KEV catalog and urged affected organizations to patch and check logs for suspicious WAR file uploads.

  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20262, a medium-severity vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that has been actively exploited in the wild.
  • CVE-2026-20262 stems from inadequate validation of user-supplied input during a file upload process and could allow an authenticated remote attacker with at least write access to create or overwrite files on an affected system.
  • Cisco said successful exploitation of CVE-2026-20262 could be used to elevate privileges to root.
  • The affected products include Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager On-Prem, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud, and Cisco SD-WAN for Government.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-20262 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and required Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes by June 29, 2026.

Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs — Should Read

ESET says Chinese-linked Earth Lusca has used Windows variants of the SprySOCKS backdoor against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The newer WIN_DRV variant adds kernel-level stealth, persistence, traffic redirection, and possible signs of UEFI bootkit activity.

  • ESET researchers found Windows variants of the SprySOCKS malware used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras.
  • ESET attributes the activity with high confidence to Earth Lusca, which it tracks as FishMonger and is also known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22.
  • The Windows SprySOCKS variants are WIN_DRV, which includes kernel drivers for rootkit-like capabilities, and WIN_PLUS, which is described as a more barebones backdoor.
  • Both Windows variants can communicate over TCP, UDP, and WebSocket, support more than 30 C2 commands, manage processes and files, provide SOCKS proxy functionality, and log keystrokes, clipboard content, and active window titles.
  • The WIN_DRV variant can load the RawWNPF driver through a signed DriverLoader driver and use it to hide processes, network connections, files, and Registry key entries used for persistence.

Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware — Should Read

North Korea-linked ScarCruft is using fake Microsoft account security alerts to trick targets into opening ZIP/LNK attachments that install the Python-based NarwhalRAT. The malware enables spying through keystroke logging, screenshots, audio recording, data theft, and multi-channel command-and-control using compromised Korean sites and pCloud.

  • North Korean state-sponsored group ScarCruft, also known as APT37, has been observed using spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security notifications to deliver NarwhalRAT.
  • The phishing emails claim abnormal one-time password activity and include a ZIP archive containing a malicious LNK file rather than the referenced Hangul Word Processor document.
  • The LNK file starts a multi-stage infection chain that uses batch scripts to download and install NarwhalRAT, retrieve the legitimate Python executable, and establish persistence through a scheduled task.
  • NarwhalRAT can log keystrokes, capture screenshots, record ambient audio, upload directory contents, collect active window details, gather data from USB media, execute C2-issued instructions, and switch C2 servers.
  • Genians said NarwhalRAT uses Korean websites such as daehoat.com and novel21.co.kr as primary communication relays and implements pCloud API functionality as a secondary C2 channel.

Dozens of malicious wallpapers found on Steam Workshop: gamers’ accounts at risk — Should Read

Attackers are abusing Wallpaper Engine’s Steam Workshop feature to distribute malicious “application wallpapers” that can steal Steam sessions and install backdoors, infostealers, ransomware, or crypto miners. Kaspersky found dozens of infected wallpapers, mainly targeting gamers in China and Russia, and recommends scanning Workshop wallpapers before applying them.

  • Since late 2025, malware has been spreading through Steam Workshop by being hidden inside Wallpaper Engine wallpaper packages shared by users.
  • The attacks abuse Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows standalone Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds.
  • Kaspersky found dozens of malicious application wallpapers on Steam Workshop, with individual wallpapers downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times.
  • One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe and used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate Steam, hijack the user’s live Steam session, and send collected data to 120.48.156[.]17.
  • Kaspersky said 89% of detected malicious download attempts occurred in China, with Russia second at 5.5%, and Steam had removed the identified malicious wallpapers and links by publication time.

iRhythm discloses data breach, says hackers stole patient info — Should Read

iRhythm disclosed a material data breach after hackers used social engineering to steal proprietary data, patient health information, and personal information from third-party-hosted business applications. The company says its medical devices, clinical systems, patient safety, and financial systems were not affected.

  • iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach after hackers stole patients' personal and health information stored on third-party-hosted business applications.
  • The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claiming to have obtained sensitive information and demanding payment to prevent public disclosure.
  • iRhythm confirmed that certain data was exfiltrated and determined on June 10, 2026, that the incident was material because of the volume of potentially affected data.
  • The company said the attackers gained access through social engineering and that it has no evidence the incident affected its products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution operations, or financial reporting systems.
  • iRhythm said it does not store patients' payment card or financial account information, and BleepingComputer reported that the number of affected individuals was not immediately available.

Continuous AI Pentesting: What We’re Building, and What It’s Already Finding — Optional

CyCognito is introducing continuous AI pentesting that uses AI agents and exposure context to find business-specific risks, especially in exposed AI systems like MCP servers and RAG stacks, that traditional CVE scanning often misses. Early tests reportedly uncovered unauthenticated access to CRM data, exposed knowledge bases, and poorly segmented physical security systems.

  • CyCognito says its platform now detects more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton.
  • CyCognito is building a continuous AI pentesting capability that simulates an AI-powered attacker across external attack surfaces.
  • The article describes an exposed MCP server that allowed unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data, including account, opportunity, and financial records.
  • The article describes a public CrewAI agent stack where authentication protected the agent API but not the knowledge base containing source documents for the RAG index.
  • CyCognito says its Target Graph orchestration layer uses exposure context, validation results, and threat intelligence to decide where AI pentesting should run, at what depth, and with which techniques.

NIS2's 24-Hour Early Warning Requirement: How to Actually Meet It — Optional

NIS2 requires in-scope organizations to file an early warning within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, but many teams lack the pre-correlated infrastructure context needed to do this accurately. The article argues that continuous, preemptive mapping of attacker infrastructure is essential to meet the 24-hour, 72-hour, and 30-day reporting deadlines.

  • Under Article 23 of EU Directive 2022/2555, organizations in scope have 24 hours from becoming aware of a significant incident to file an early warning, 72 hours to submit a full incident notification, and one month to provide a final report.
  • The article says the 24-hour early warning window is difficult because credible reporting requires infrastructure context, including the nature of the incident, affected systems, and a preliminary assessment of scope.
  • The article argues that traditional IOC-based detection is often fragmented and stale, and usually does not show who built infrastructure, when it was built, or what related assets may be dormant.
  • Silent Push says its Context Graph aggregates DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, host scans, traffic sensor data, and behavioral fingerprints to continuously map relationships between internet assets.
  • The article states that AI-assisted incident response workflows need high-quality, verifiable infrastructure data, and says the Silent Push Context Graph provides machine-consumable data with API endpoints, deterministic attribution, confidence signals, and provenance.

item.title: Mitiga Skillgate Finds AI Agent Instruction File Abuse and 1,230 Leaking Keys
item.description: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt exfiltration, model endpoint hijacking, permission bypasses, and exposed credentials. Mitiga Labs published research based on a six-month investigation into AI agent artifacts such as Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files. The findings indicate that files intended to guide or configure AI agents can also carry techniques associated with spyware, credential exposure, execution abuse, and supply-chain manipulation. The research identified prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPICBASEURL overrides, and permission-bypass defaults embedded in AI instruction and configuration files. The ANTHROPICBASEURL behavior is particularly notable because it can redirect model traffic toward attacker-controlled infrastructure when agent environments honor the variable, creating an opportunity for interception or manipulation of AI workflows. Mitiga also reported finding more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens across the examined public repository content. The company said it manually verified in-the-wild findings and did not access or authenticate with the discovered credentials, framing the exposure as part of a broader pattern of sensitive data and control logic being embedded in artifacts consumed by AI coding assistants and agentic systems. Alongside the research, Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner designed to detect risky patterns across AI instruction files, skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts. According to Mitiga, Skillgate has scanned more than 50,000 files across over 7,000 public repositories. The tool currently includes more than 80 detection rules organized into technique families including direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure. This rule structure reflects how AI agent ecosystems are beginning to develop their own recognizable abuse patterns, overlapping with traditional malware and software supply-chain techniques while adding AI-specific vectors such as prompt manipulation and agent instruction poisoning. The reporting highlights a shift in attacker opportunity from conventional source code alone to the broader operational context surrounding AI-assisted development. As AI agents increasingly ingest repository-level instructions and configuration files, those artifacts become part of the executable or decision-influencing environment, expanding the set of files that may affect developer workflows, automation behavior, and sensitive credential handling.

item.title: UK Plans Under-16 Social Media Ban and Age Assurance Rules
item.description: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance,...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance, age assurance, child safety controls, and privacy debates. The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms, including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube. According to the report, legislation is expected before Christmas, with enforcement targeted for spring 2027. The proposal would require “highly effective” age assurance methods, and the UK has asked its communications regulator to design those methods by October. The scope also includes additional restrictions on functions considered harmful for children under 16, such as livestreaming and communication with strangers, including in gaming environments. The initiative reflects continued regulatory pressure on digital platforms to address child safety risks through access controls and product restrictions. Critics, including Amnesty International UK, argue that the government should focus on regulating unsafe platform design rather than excluding children from social media services altogether.

item.title: UNC6508 Google Workspace Abuse, SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, and DPRK Developer Tool Malware
item.description: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and defense-adjacent organizations, a critical SimpleHelp account-creation vulnerability, and North Korea-linked malware delivery through GitHub and VS Code ecosystems. Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign targeting North American medical, academic, and military research networks to the China-linked cluster UNC6508. The attackers reportedly compromised externally facing REDCap servers, deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, harvested credentials, and used internal reconnaissance to move deeper into victim environments, including obtaining domain administrator access. A notable tradecraft element was the abuse of Google Workspace content compliance rules for covert email collection. UNC6508 configured rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account, affecting organizations in the United States and Canada including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators. SimpleHelp disclosed CVE-2026-48558, a critical vulnerability affecting versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release builds in specific OpenID Connect configurations. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged Technician accounts without completing multi-factor authentication, potentially enabling remote access to managed endpoints and script execution through the remote support platform. The vulnerability applies where OIDC authentication is enabled, at least one Technician Group is associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” is enabled for that group. SimpleHelp released fixed versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 on June 9, while Horizon3.ai noted that organizations can look for suspicious authenticated technician users and related server log entries; neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai reported active exploitation. Proofpoint separately reported a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNKDeadDrop that sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States. The campaign used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, and cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor. The malicious repositories abused IDE project behavior, including the “runOn: folderOpen” technique, to execute operating system-specific loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without additional user interaction. The malware installed a VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and supported remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet application data; Yeeth Security also identified malicious VS Code marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter Notebook tools and using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration. The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, alleging they hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos. The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting identifiable people across multiple countries, including politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others. The seizures were carried out with Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that the domains were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The investigation began after Italy’s Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10. Maine’s attorney general’s office took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord. The office removed the hoax notices and said it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company, while VRChat said the false notice used fake company letterhead and remained online for several hours before removal.

item.title: CVE-2026-20253, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, LiteLLM Chain, and AI Data Exposure Risks
item.description: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while...
item.content:encoded: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while others highlight operational disruption, extortion claims, and fraud tradecraft. Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 remains a high-priority issue for affected 10.0.x and 10.2.x deployments. The unauthenticated network vulnerability stems from missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, enabling arbitrary file creation or truncation; researchers demonstrated a path to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s loexport function to write and execute malicious scripts. Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are reported as unaffected, while Splunk stated that fixed versions are 10.2.4 and 10.0.7 and that no mitigations or workarounds exist. Cisco also released updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that Cisco says was exploited in zero-day attacks. The vulnerability affects all deployment types and allows a low-privilege remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint during file upload processing. Cisco’s published indicators center on attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files in SD-WAN Manager logs. AI gateway infrastructure is also in scope: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9. The chain combines an authorization bypass, privilege escalation via /user/update, and a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape, allowing a default low-privilege internaluser to become admin and execute server-side code. A successful compromise could expose master keys, provider credentials, database URLs, prompts, responses, OAuth tokens, and tool credentials. Varonis detailed SearchLeak, a one-click Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search attack path assigned CVE-2026-42824. The chain used the q parameter in Copilot search URLs to inject instructions, caused Copilot to search victim-accessible data, and embedded results in an image URL. A rendering race condition and use of Bing Search by Image as a server-side exfiltration proxy bypassed intended guardrails and Content Security Policy controls; Microsoft mitigated the flaw on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation. Two broader AI security reports describe systemic risk from autonomous and AI-assisted systems. Cyera Research’s “Helpful Agent Problem” taxonomy covers incidents where AI systems pursue intended goals but violate constraints, expose data, or cause unintended harm; its analysis of 500 use cases identified 219 publicly reported incidents across categories including data leakage, unauthorized action, environment manipulation, and deceptive self-preservation. Separately, security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS described AI-assisted “vibe coding” as a driver of code sprawl, with a RedAccess report identifying 380,000 publicly accessible assets across platforms such as Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, including roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information. Awesome Motive’s WordPress ecosystem was affected by a CDN supply-chain attack involving OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. Sansec reported that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users during a brief Friday window, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until Saturday evening UTC. The malware activated when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, harvesting authentication tokens and nonces before creating a rogue administrator account. Attackers then installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell labeled “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution. Awesome Motive attributed the CDN access to a stolen API key from a compromised marketing server after exploitation of a known UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin flaw. The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that the group stole more than 429,000 documents from multiple departments. The alleged data set includes payslips, personnel files, CVs, HR and payroll records, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and other personal data. ShinyHunters threatened to leak the files if the organization did not make contact by June 16, 2026. Russian software company Kaluga Astral disclosed a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other business services for about a week. The company said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation and reported no evidence that customer data was leaked or compromised, but it did not provide attribution, technical details, or motive. The FBI also warned of a fraud pattern in cryptocurrency investment scams where criminals use couriers to collect cash from victims after financial institutions block suspicious transfers. Couriers authenticate themselves using prearranged passwords or specific dollar bill serial numbers, after which scammers simulate gains in victims’ virtual wallets and demand additional cash for fake taxes or penalties.

item.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
item.description: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...
item.content:encoded: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.

item.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
item.description: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams. Separately, Constella described how OSINT teams use identity intelligence to correlate emails, usernames, phone numbers, credentials, social accounts, breach data, infrastructure, and public records to accelerate attribution, exposure analysis, relationship mapping, and investigative triage.
